title: Jet Skis and Water Toys on a Charter: A Practical Guide to What Is Included
topic: Jet skis and water toys: what to expect on a charter
html:
Half the fun of a day on the water happens once the engines are off and the swim ladder is down. Jet skis, paddleboards, inflatable slides and snorkelling kit can turn a quiet anchorage into the highlight of a trip. Yet water toys vary enormously from one boat to the next, and assumptions about what comes as standard often lead to disappointment. Before you rent a boat for a weekend or a fortnight, it helps to understand what these extras include, what they cost, and which rules tend to apply once you are afloat.

What counts as a water toy

The phrase covers a wide range of equipment, from simple to powerful. At the gentle end you have snorkel sets, masks, towable rings, kayaks and stand-up paddleboards. In the middle sit inflatable platforms, floating mats and water trampolines that clip to the stern. At the lively end are motorised items such as jet skis, sea bobs, electric foils and tow-behind toys pulled by the tender. Each category carries different requirements for storage, fuel, insurance and supervision, which is why no two charters offer exactly the same selection.

What is usually included, and what is not

On most bareboat and crewed charters, light non-motorised gear tends to be standard or available for a small fee. Paddleboards, kayaks and snorkelling equipment rarely add much to the price. Motorised toys are a different story. A jet ski almost always sits on a separate line in the quote, partly because of fuel and partly because of the insurance it requires. When you compare options for boat rental, read the inclusions list carefully rather than relying on photos, since a jet ski pictured on deck may be an optional add-on rather than part of the base price.

A few items worth checking before you book:

  • Fuel for motorised toys, which is often billed separately from the boat itself.
  • Quantity, since one paddleboard between eight guests creates a queue.
  • Condition and age, especially for inflatables that puncture and pumps that fail.
  • Deposits, as some operators ask for a security hold against damage to expensive gear.

Licences, age limits and local rules

Jet skis attract the most regulation. In many countries you need a personal watercraft licence or a recreational boating qualification to operate one, and minimum age limits commonly start at sixteen or eighteen. Some destinations restrict jet ski use to designated zones, ban them near beaches, or require a guide to accompany you. A reputable owner will brief you on these rules, but the responsibility for staying within them usually rests with the hirer. If you plan to hire a boat specifically for its jet ski, confirm in writing that you are permitted to drive it at your chosen location, because a toy you cannot legally use is no toy at all.

Crewed versus bareboat: how it changes the experience

On a crewed yacht charter the crew typically handle launching, fuelling and safety, which means you simply ask and the toys appear. This is the most relaxed way to enjoy a sea bob or a towed ring, since someone else manages the logistics and watches the water while guests play. On a bareboat charter you are the operator, so you inflate, deploy and store everything yourself, and you carry the safety duty too. Neither is better, they suit different trips. Families with young children often prefer a crewed yacht charter for the extra supervision, while confident sailors enjoy the freedom of doing it all themselves.

Safety and etiquette on the water

Whatever the kit, a few habits keep everyone happy. Always brief first-timers on how a jet ski accelerates, since the throttle response surprises newcomers. Keep buoyancy aids on for towed toys and for children at all times. Stay well clear of swimmers, other boats and the anchor chain when riding anything motorised, and cut the engine before anyone re-boards. Respect quiet anchorages by limiting engine noise at dawn and dusk. Good etiquette is not only courteous, it reduces the chance of an accident that ends the day early.

How to find the right boat for your toys

The simplest approach is to filter for equipment when you search. Decide which toys matter most to your group, then shortlist vessels that list them clearly and state the terms. It pays to browse boats across a few destinations, since a marina an hour away may offer better gear for the same budget. If you own a vessel and your equipment is in good order, you can list your boat and highlight those extras to attract guests who care about more than the deck size.

Water toys can lift an ordinary outing into a memorable one, provided expectations are set before departure. Ask what is included, confirm the rules, and match the boat to how active your group wants to be. Do that, and the swim stop becomes the part of the trip everyone talks about afterwards.
